title: Logon ('L')
text: Logon ('L') Log on to the DBMS. Description Log on to the DBMS (initialize a session with the DBMS). If the DBMS does not support Logon, return 0 to Uniface. A successful Logon results in a logon path (in Uniface terminology). It is sometimes called a channel, logon session, DBMS process, or connection in DBMS terminology. If the Logon was successful, or if a session was successfully initialized, provide Uniface with a number to uniquely identify the created logon path. Update the number of open files and open cursors. If the DBMS supports a transaction mechanism and requires an explicit ‘Begin Transaction’, this must be provided. If Logon is requested when the maximum number of simultaneous logon paths is already open, return -1 to Uniface. If a logon path is TM-controlled ( uctrl->TMEnabled=1 ), the DBMS connector performs the internal administration and configuration, but it must not actually log on to the DBMS. From Uniface Available Uniface information for the Logon request. Table 1. Information Available from Uniface Data Structure->Element Description uctrl->umisc Points to database name, user name and password separated by the ‘|’ character and must be terminated by a NULL character ( /0 in C): database|user|password/0 Any of the three parts can be empty. Normally the separators and terminator are present, but you should exercise caution. If the connector-specified uinfo->umode = 0 in the Info request, indicating that a database name, user name and password are not required, Uniface does not process the logon information but passes the logon string to the connector through uctrl->umisc. Note that such a logon string may not conform to the syntax given above. uctrl->iobuf Points to the general I/O buffer. uctrl->sqlbuf Points to the SQL buffer. uctrl->TMEnabled Indicates whether the connector is TM-controlled. uctrl->udrvini This field is not meaningful in the Logon request. To Uniface Connector request return values related to the Logon request. Table 2. Connector Request Return Values Value Description 0 Logon was successful. -1 Logon failed, exceptional error. -16 DBMS reported network error. If the connector request return value is less than 0, an error code must be supplied in uctrl->udrverr . Service Functions udfilcnt — Update the number of open files. udpathset — Provide Uniface with a number that uniquely identifies the created logon path. Connectors that require a unique value in uctrl->udrvini must call udpathset even when the connector is TM-controlled. udcurscnt — Update the number of open cursors. Extra information The Logon request is provided with a dummy control block. This control block does not describe a table and it does not have an associated connector workspace. The connection identification numbers are passed to the udpathset and should be larger than zero. Some DBMSs return a ‘logon handle’. This is needed to identify the logon path when the database is accessed or logged off. Uniface does not allocate buffer space to store these handles. The connector must use the operating system’s dynamic memory allocation, or define a fixed static space. The connector is fully responsible for maintaining the table of logon handles. The logon handle table is a mapping of the unique identification for this DBMS onto the unique identification understood by Uniface. The logon handle scheme described above is simplified in the following situations: If the DBMS does not support the concept of different logon paths, the connector can respond to a Logon request in one of the following ways: Always supply the same fixed-path identification number to Uniface and return 0. This approach ignores multiple Logon requests. Use a flag (initialized to FALSE) to indicate that the connector is already logged on to the DBMS. At a Logon request, if the flag is set, return -1 to Uniface; otherwise, supply a fixed logon path identification number to Uniface, set the flag to TRUE and return 0 to Uniface. The flag must be reset if a Logoff request is successfully executed. Using this approach, the DBMS is treated as a DBMS with a maximum number of simultaneous open logon paths of 1. If the DBMS uses simple logon handles, a computed mapping may be implemented onto the Uniface logon path identifier. This would eliminate the need for a table and table access functions. TM-Controlled Connectors Connectors that do not provide TM support do not need to be modified ( uinfo->TMCapable = 0). TM-controlled connectors must perform the standard internal administration and configuration, but the connector must not connect to the DBMS, because the TM has already logged on to the DBMS. The Logon connector request is used by some TM-controlled DBMS connectors (for example Oracle) to get the logon handle for the DBMS connection. If specific DBMS configuration settings are required and are not set by the TM, the DBMS connector must set them. Connectors that must call pathset , ucurscnt and ufilcnt when they are not TM-controlled, must also perform these calls when TM-controlled. TM-controlled DBMS connectors are not allowed to begin local transactions after the Logon request completes. Some connectors must use a local transaction during the Logon request, and this local transaction must be ended in the Logon request. Related Links uctrl Exchanges general information between Uniface and the connector, and describes a table. TM-Controlled DBMS Connectors A database connector can be controlled by a transaction manager only if it is TM-capable. Uniface does not provide TM-controlled connectors and does not directly support third-party transaction managers. However, you can use the APIs provided by the Database Connector Interface to build a custom database connector that does. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
